quotes: I worked at a market research firm, and my main part of the job was to ensure that over 500 documents got uploaded correctly to their website. Each document was a description of each product that they could provide, and I needed to make sure that all the formatting was correct. There were no grammar errors that everything just looked consistent across the platform. So sometimes there would be incorrect fonts, or there would be something that was bolded that shouldn't be bolded as an example.
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: Emily's only described experience is checking non-educational product description documents at a market research firm for visual formatting consistency — incorrect fonts, bolding errors, grammar. She has no educational content background, no curriculum or teaching experience, and no experience reviewing instructional or structured learning materials for content accuracy. Per the rubric's hard-fail definition, a candidate whose only review experience is checking non-educational documents for visual formatting consistency, with no educational content background whatsoever, does not meet the core bar. This is a hard fail on Role Fit.

quotes: I would go through it line by line to make sure that all of the formatting was correct, as well as the spelling and the grammar.
sometimes there would be incorrect fonts, or there would be something that was bolded that shouldn't be bolded
domain_judgment.rationale: Emily's entire conception of error types is limited to formatting, spelling, grammar, and visual consistency. She names no content-accuracy error categories, no curriculum-level concerns, no functional or accessibility issues, and shows no awareness that different error types carry different severity. Her example error is a bolding inconsistency. This is textbook score-1 domain judgment — treating QA as proofreading.

quotes: I would keep my process consistent by taking my time and working off a checklist to make sure that I wasn't missing anything that needed to be checked.
I would just update the spreadsheet, and I would email the editing team and let them know this is what needs to be done, and I would continue to follow up until the errors were corrected.
I would obviously just take care of the ones that I knew I would be able to fix, perhaps ask a colleague, bounce an idea of them to confirm that what I was doing was correct
process_and_methodology.rationale: Emily mentions a checklist and a tracking spreadsheet, which shows some structural awareness. She also describes a follow-up loop with the editing team. However, she provides no detail about what the checklist contains, no multi-pass sequence, no named steps, and no articulated logic for escalation beyond 'take care of what I can fix.' The process is loose and underdeveloped — a score of 2 is appropriate.

quotes: I would definitely take my time, I would not rush through it, I would go through it line by line
I would make sure that everything was detail oriented, listed out every single issue, and every single item that needed to be done, so that way they wouldn't have any questions
communication.rationale: Emily's answers are followable and organized. She addresses each question in sequence, stays on topic, and lands on a clear point in each answer. The delivery is conversational but not rambling. The substance is thin, but the communication itself is clear enough to track without effort. Score 3 is appropriate — the weakness is in content depth, not communication clarity.

quotes: And then the last question asks when you find an issue...
Tell us your experience about catching content or quality errors...
Thanks so much for reviewing my video, and I look forward to hearing from you soon.
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: Emily answered all three questions in order, maintained a professional tone throughout, and closed appropriately. However, her answers lack depth and specificity — particularly Q2 and Q3 — so she does not reach the bar for a 4. She met the baseline expectation of coverage and professionalism.

recommendation_reason: Hard fail: candidate lacks core experience for the role.
overall_summary: Emily is a hard-fail candidate. Her only described QA experience is checking non-educational product description documents at a market research firm for visual formatting consistency (fonts, bolding, grammar). She has no educational content background, no teaching or curriculum experience, and no experience reviewing instructional materials for content accuracy. This places her squarely in the automatic-decline category per the rubric's core bar definition. Beyond the hard fail, her domain judgment is limited to surface-level formatting errors, and her process description is thin. Communication is adequate. There are no transcript quality issues that would reduce confidence in this evaluation.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
